data.title: Occidental Petroleum: Concentrate On The Acquisitions Made
data.description:
There was a recent announcement about the coming management changes with Vicki Hollub, President and CEO of Occidental Petroleum (OXY), retiring. But short of a major malfunction in this process, usually it takes several years for a new person to “make his mark” on a big company like this one. Therefore, while it is important for the new CEO to begin establishing a new record right away, it is probably much more important to notice the effects of the acquisitions on the company's cost structure in the future.

Many of the acquisitions (if not all of them) were “sold” with the idea that they were accretive right away. The last article covered this in some detail. That statement is fair enough and should have already been judged by the market and individual investors. However, a low-cost acquisition also has future benefits in that lower-cost wells slowly gain in significance from these acquisitions to keep lowering the company's overall breakeven point over time.

Furthermore, remember that Occidental Petroleum sold the chemical division to Berkshire Hathaway Inc. (BRK.A) (BRK.B). That took care of any debt issues from the CrownRock acquisition (or really any of the acquisition period). Management several times expected many of these acquisitions to have in the neighborhood of 20% lower costs (especially with the improvements that management had in mind). That is a very rough number that may be exceeded in some cases while possibly falling short in other cases.

The other thing is that technology keeps advancing to make these deals even better. The significance and speed of those advances can always be a discussion point. But over time, they become a big part of the overall strategy.

There is still the preferred stock to deal with from the Anadarko acquisition. But that should be a very straightforward process now that the sale of the Chemical business has been completed. Of course, it also helps that commodity prices have spiked with the Iranian situation.

This often becomes very evident when commodity prices climb (like right now) in the form of greater-than-expected profitability or at least expected improved profitability. Lowering costs is often a weighted average process in that the lower costs become more significant each year. Therefore, the full value of the acquisitions made in the past will become evident over time.

But often the market cares about now, which can be confusing to investors. Many do not realize that the market will respond to outperformance or good news later. So, wise managements often talk about what will happen on an accretive basis (meaning over the following year) while making sure there is more good news to come over time.

The first acquisition, the Anadarko acquisition, took about 3 years for investors to see the full benefits in fiscal year 2022. The fact that the lower costs of this acquisition helped tremendously in the pandemic (challenges) is really overshadowed by the low prices that made it hard for investors to evaluate much of anything. Clearly, what dominated the analysis at the time was the extremely low and even negative commodity prices, combined with a very uncertain future.

Since then, there have been still more acquisitions to further enhance the cost structure of the company. It needs to be understood that this company was so big that really only a major discovery, a large improvement, or significant acquisitions were going to change things. It would appear that Occidental went for “all of the above.” Even now, Occidental still concentrates to a large extent on the latest technology improvements to maintain its position near the top of competitive abilities due to great geology.

What the acquisitions did was add some lower-cost production in a quantity that would affect the corporate average right away. This may have been initially offset with choices made to put as much of the acquisition costs in depreciable assets and then use very conservative assumptions to depreciate as much of the old production as quickly as allowed.

Those are all non-cash choices that protect the cash flow by keeping taxes on reported production as low as possible in the beginning. This is important because one-time assimilation costs often require extra cash flow that comes from the high depreciation.

Once everything is optimal, then the cost-lowering process begins, and it often takes several years to become significant to investors due to that weighted average process. Older costs from a less efficient operation often have to be lived with until production declines to the point where it does not make much of a difference anymore. Sometimes reworks speed up the process. But it is very hard for investors to count on that idea becoming significant enough.

So, the new person succeeding Vicki Hollub will likely have as a main purpose the continuing optimization that was begun by her. That kind of makes it hard to go wrong in the beginning (even though I have seen a few unfortunate individuals go wrong over the years). What really happens is that a bit further down the road, there will be significant choices for the new leader to make. When that happens, we will learn the quality of the new CEO (for whom an assumption has been made, who will be Richard Jackson. He is currently the COO).

However, there has yet to be a formal announcement of the succession. Note that the article I referenced does name someone. But until someone is formally selected, it is best not to “fall in love” with that future pick until that future pick is named. More than once, there has been a “surprise” pick.

It should also be noted that Occidental has been very fortunate in that there has been a history of fairly strong leaders at the top of the company. However, a large company like this one is very likely to have a mediocre pick at some point. When that happens, a strong board will fix things promptly to minimize any potential damage. On the other hand, a passive board will let things go (sometimes for far too long). It is therefore important for investors to evaluate the training the new person has had before taking the job, as well as the board of directors itself.

In any business, mistakes can be made. What counts is how fast they are fixed and fixed correctly. Furthermore, what counts is knowing there will be some decisions made that do not work out. Given that some decisions do not work out, the right decisions have to have more effect than the wrong ones.

The commodity industry is very volatile and has low visibility. Therefore, agility and humility are key characteristics of a successful leader. You cannot fall in love with a decision to the point of blind loyalty. That is a very good way to lose money in a fast-moving industry like this one.

Summary

Occidental Petroleum remains a strong buy in that the acquisitions are likely to have still more benefits coming than the market may realize. That could very well mean greater profitability at each pricing point that might show as record profitability (compared to the past) when commodity prices rise, as in the current situation. Now those higher prices need to last long enough for the benefits to become apparent to the market. But sooner or later, that is a very good bet to happen in an extremely volatile and low-visibility industry like this one.

Risks

Sometimes it takes years for the market to realize the benefits of an acquisition. The Anadarko acquisition took 3 years, according to the article that I referenced. Some still dispute its effectiveness.

What made it more complicated is that the market back in 2022 valued the record earnings at much less than it valued earnings before fiscal year 2020, when many were predicting an eventual $200 common stock price (which, of course, never happened). That makes valuing future benefits a real challenge whenever a valuation of the earnings process can change.

The whole industry is no longer considered a growth industry. This has resulted in demands for investors' returns (such as dividends and stock repurchases) while restricting growth plans. There are likely other changes that are not as apparent to investors (like a lower debt ratio required by the debt market and “living within cash flow”).

Despite a reported training program for the next job, there is no guarantee that the next CEO will be successful. In that case, fixing the situation promptly will be important.

data.title: Commodities: Oil Steadies As Iran Deadline Pushed Back But Upside Risks Persist
data.description:
By Ewa Manthey, Commodities Strategist | Warren Patterson, Head of Commodities Strategy

Energy – Oil steadies as Iran deadline is pushed back

Oil prices steadied after US President Donald Trump again pushed back the deadline for striking Iran’s energy. Trump said Tehran had requested a seven‑day extension, but he opted for 10 days, setting a new deadline of April 6.

Brent was trading around $108 a barrel, while West Texas Intermediate was near $94 on Friday morning.

Extending the ceasefire takes some near-term heat out of the market, but risks still lean to the upside. The scale of supply at risk remains significant – around 8 million barrels per day are already offline, and a much larger volume of flows through the Gulf remains vulnerable – so the geopolitical premium is unlikely to fade meaningfully.

Thursday saw another volatile session in the oil market; prices rose following conflicting signals from Washington and Tehran. President Trump said he didn't know if the US is “willing” to work with Iran on a deal, shortly after the US warned of potential threats from Iran‑based Houthi militants in the Bab el‑Mandeb Strait. Prices had briefly pared gains after reports that Iran had responded to a US‑backed 15‑point peace proposal via intermediaries, though Tehran has previously rejected US outreach and continues to push its own conditions, including proposals to formalise transit fees for the Strait of Hormuz, with lawmakers working on a draft bill to impose a toll in exchange for providing security to ships via the key waterway.

With both sides continuing attacks and the US reportedly reinforcing its military presence in the region, concerns over supply disruptions remain elevated.

Meanwhile, Iran permitted Malaysian vessels trapped in the Gulf to return home through the strait, Malaysia’s Prime Minister Anwar Ibrahim said on Thursday evening.

Trump also said on Thursday that Iran had allowed 10 oil tankers to sail through the strait as a goodwill gesture. An insurance programme meant to boost shipping through the strait would also begin soon, says US Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent.

For the LNG market, supply risks have intensified after a tropical cyclone forced production cuts at three Australian LNG plants, together accounting for around 8% of global supply. The disruptions come on top of earlier shocks from the closure of the Strait of Hormuz and the shutdown of Qatar’s largest liquefaction facility following attacks, further tightening an already strained market and increasing price pressure for Asian buyers.

In Europe, ARA refined product inventories fell by 115kt week-on-week to 5.3mt in the week to 26 March, according to Insights Global, driven by declines in gasoline (‑75kt), naphtha (‑45kt) and fuel oil (‑13kt). Gasoil stocks rose by 57kt to 2.15mt, though middle‑distillate cracks remain well-supported amid ongoing uncertainty, with the ICE gasoil crack holding above $50/bbl this morning.

Singapore refined product inventories rose sharply by 2.2mb WoW to 52mb – the highest level since December 2024 – led by builds in middle distillates (+1.23mb) and light distillates (+0.5mb). Residual fuel stocks also increased by 471kb to 24.5mb over the week.

US natural gas prices extended gains, with front‑month Henry Hub futures approaching $3/MMBtu, after storage draws exceeded expectations. EIA data showed inventories fell by 54Bcf last week, well above the five‑year average draw of 21Bcf, leaving stocks at 1.829Tcf – just 0.8% above the seasonal average.

Metals – Iran war keeps metals on edge

Copper climbed on Friday and was on track for its first weekly gain this month after President Trump extended the deadline for Iran to strike a deal, lifting hopes of de‑escalation and supporting growth sentiment.

Most industrial metals have fallen this month as uncertainty around US-Iran negotiations and the prolonged conflict – now approaching a one-month mark – keeps risk sentiment fragile.

Heightened geopolitical tensions have raised concerns about inflation while also reinforcing fears of slowing industrial activity globally, weighing on demand expectations. Against this backdrop, copper prices have fallen around 7% so far this month, reflecting a broader reassessment of growth-linked exposure across the base metals complex.

Meanwhile, aluminium prices remain supported, with supply risks from a sustained Strait of Hormuz closure – already prompting production curbs – offsetting concerns over weaker demand.

In other base metals, zinc prices rose around 1% on Thursday after larger-than-expected supply disruptions at Boliden’s Garpenberg mine in Sweden. The company said it will run the mine at 30% capacity until further notice, following a period of abnormally high seismic activity.

In precious metals, Turkey’s central bank sold and swapped around 60 tonnes of gold, worth over $8bn, in the two weeks following the start of the Iran war, with reserves falling by six tonnes in the week to 13 March and a further 52.4 tonnes the following week. While some gold was sold outright, the bulk was used in swap transactions to secure foreign currency or lira liquidity, according to Bloomberg, marking a sharp reversal for one of the world’s most aggressive gold buyers over the past decade.

Official‑sector buying has been a central pillar of gold’s rally over the past couple of years.

Since the war began, gold has fallen more than 15%, moving largely inversely with oil prices as rising energy costs have lifted inflation expectations and pushed markets to price in a higher‑for‑longer rates outlook. This dynamic has limited gold’s ability to act as a geopolitical hedge, with firmer real yields and a resilient US dollar offsetting safe‑haven demand.

Agriculture - US crop plantings estimate

The USDA will release its prospective plantings report next week, with a Bloomberg survey pointing to US corn acreage of 94.5m acres in 2026, slightly above the prior estimate but still 4.4% below last season. Corn plantings are expected to decline as farmers shift towards soybeans following ample supplies from the record 2025 harvest. Soybean acreage is seen rising to 85.5m acres, up from 81.2m acres last year, despite ongoing trade tensions with China and strong competition from Brazil. Wheat plantings are projected to edge lower to 44.7m acres, down from last year’s 45.3m acres.

Meanwhile, cocoa supply risks are rising in the Ivory Coast after a farmers’ group threatened to halt bean deliveries to ports from 1 April unless unsold stockpiles are cleared. Farmers are holding around 60kt of beans, while total main‑crop stocks are estimated at roughly 200kt – a backdrop that could continue to support cocoa prices.

The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

data.title: BlackRock Global Allocation V.I. Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Commentary as of 12/31/25

  • The fund posted a return of 3.43% (Class I shares) for the fourth quarter of 2025, while its reference benchmark 4 returned 2.72%.
  • The fund had an overweight position in equities (5%) and an underweight exposure to fixed income (-14%), with the rest held in commodity-related securities (4%) and cash equivalents (5%). In equities, it had overweight holdings in the communication services, information technology (IT), consumer discretionary, and financials sectors, and underweight exposures to the materials and real estate sectors. The fund had an overweight position in the United States, and underweight holdings in Japan, Canada, Australia, and Europe.
  • In fixed income, it had an underweight allocation to developed market government bonds and overweight holdings in corporate credit, securitized debt, and bank loans. Total portfolio duration (interest rate sensitivity) was in line with the benchmark at 2.2 years (total portfolio duration assumes equity duration of 0). The fund had an overweight exposure to the Japanese yen and underweight holdings in the Chinese yuan and euro.

Contributors

Detractors
In equities, security selection in IT boosted returns, though this was partially offset by an overweight exposure to the sector. Security selection and an underweight position in the financials sector were additive. Positioning in index-related futures, which were used to tactically manage regional exposure, contributed, as did the allocation to commodities, notably gold-related securities. In equities, security selection in communication services hampered performance, though this was partially offset by an overweight allocation to the sector. Security selection and an overweight position in the consumer discretionary sector detracted. In fixed income, positioning in rate derivatives, notably along the U.S. yield curve, was a hindrance.

Further insight

Global stocks advanced during the quarter, led by European and Chinese shares. Unlike the Federal Reserve (Fed), the European Central Bank (ECB) left interest rates unchanged in December. The ECB also upgraded eurozone growth forecasts and signaled that inflation was stabilizing near 2%. In China, positive manufacturing data provided a key boost to stocks. U.S. stocks finished in positive territory, though they largely lagged their global peers. Much stronger-than-expected third-quarter U.S. GDP growth outweighed concerns about high levels of capital expenditure on artificial intelligence and a modestly weaker labor market. The Fed announced two 25 basis points (bps) rate reductions during the quarter, following September's 25bps cut. The ongoing pivot toward less restrictive monetary policy generally favored most bond sectors, particularly at the front end of the yield curve. U.S. corporate bonds were also beneficiaries of a strong U.S. economy. Emerging market sovereign bonds were particularly strong performers, boosted by resilient global economic growth, moderating local inflation, and the Fed's easier monetary policy.

data.title: AMD Vs. Nvidia: Bet On AMD The Underdog
data.description:
Introduction

Nvidia (NVDA) is probably the best company in AI today. Its growth, margins, and scale are extraordinary, and its CUDA ecosystem has created a level of network effect and the resultant customer lock-in that few companies have ever achieved.

However, if I were forced to choose between the two stocks, I would still rather own Advanced Micro Devices (AMD).

The reason is not that AMD is better. It is because Nvidia’s valuation assumes that its current unchallenged dominance will persist for a long time to come, which will require it to continue to capture a disproportionate share of the economic value created by the AI boom in the long term, an unlikely feat to accomplish.

The Business Fundamental Gap And The Valuation Disparity

Let’s begin with the numbers.

As indicated in the following table (all tables herein compiled from Seeking Alpha Peer Comparison function), AMD’s market capitalization is roughly $360 billion. Nvidia stands at approximately $4.4 trillion. That is more than a tenfold difference.

Nvidia generates about five times the revenue, roughly $215.9 billion compared to $34.6 billion for AMD during the trailing 12 months, and approximately fifteen times the cash from operations, about $102.7 billion versus $7.7 billion.

Yet the two companies employ a similar number of people—about 31,000 at AMD and 42,000 at Nvidia.

The profitability gap, as demonstrated in the table below, is even more striking. Nvidia operates with gross margins above 70% and net margins above 50%, while AMD’s corresponding figures are approximately 51% and 9.6%. Fueled by about 3 times the asset turnover rate of AMD, the return on equity of Nvidia dwarfs the corresponding measures of AMD by about 15 times.

On a per-employee basis, the difference becomes almost difficult to reconcile. Each Nvidia employee, from Mr. Jensen Huang to the last entry-level employee, corresponds to a mind-boggling $102 million in market capitalization, $5.1 million in revenue, and $2.9 million in net income, compared to the modest approximately $10 million, $1.1 million, and $140K, respectively, for AMD.

However you look at it—revenue, margins, profits, or just how much each employee is producing—Nvidia is simply in a different league right now. The contrast here makes you wonder whether they are even in the same industry.

Why Nvidia Deserves This Lead (At Least For Now)

Nvidia did not get here by only designing a better chip. It has built a platform that results in what appear to be insurmountable barriers to entry. At the center of that platform is CUDA. Developers are trained on it. Enterprise workflows depend on it. Once a company commits to CUDA, it is too sticky for it to switch because switching is technically cumbersome and operationally risky. It is like Hotel California: you can check in but cannot check out.

This is what allows Nvidia to charge premium prices and sustain margins that exceed 50% at the net income level.

The Moat of Nvidia May Not Be As Permanent

Nobody denies the advantage or the dominance of Nvidia today. However, it is equally undeniable that Mr. Market has assigned a rich premium to the stock valuation of NVDA on account of such an advantage.

The real question is: how sustainable is the advantage and the resultant premium?

Where the market may be overreaching is in treating Nvidia’s dominance as if it were inevitably permanent or even structurally stable.

Nvidia enjoys powerful advantages. But they are much more breakable in the way that trade secret-intensive and production process-based monopolies are.

You can think of Nvidia for now as the most sought-after architect firm in town. It has come up with the design everyone wants to use for AI compute. But the architects across the street, AMD, are not sitting still. They are studying that design, trying to close the gap.

There is no fundamental structural reason that they cannot. This is not like a manufacturing monopoly like TSMC or Coca-Cola, where others are locked out via trade secrets --- there are only so many people in this world who know how to manufacture a 2-nanometer (2nm)-process-node chip, or have access to the secret recipe for Coca-Cola. The gap is real, but it is not protected by a similarly unbreakable barrier.

The designs of AMD may not inspire the same “wow” yet. But buyers may not need to choose based on one factor alone.

The current dynamic between AMD and NVDA reminds me of a familiar scene: two highly competitive cousins (coincidentally, Dr. Lisa Su and Mr. Jensen Huang are distant cousins) reaching for the same prized object. Of course, the “toy” here is not your regular Barbie, but the dominance of the global market for AI compute.

The difference, of course, is that this is not a zero-sum game. The “toy” is expanding rapidly, and there may be room for more than one winner. The current winner may not be taken for granted as the forever winner that will leave the other cousin no room for success.

AMD Under Lisa Su Has Done This Before

It is almost tempting to look at the current gap between Nvidia and AMD and conclude that it is insurmountable. That conclusion would have been equally reasonable or at least appealing a decade ago when AMD was the underdog in its race against Intel.

At the time, AMD was widely viewed as an inferior competitor. At one point, it was even close to shutting down its business. Intel dominated not only in scale and profitability, but also in ecosystem and customer relationships. Sounds familiar, huh?

Under the leadership of Dr. Lisa Su, AMD not only caught up with Intel, but reversed that position. Of course, Intel helped AMD along with its own bloated bureaucracy and loss of long-term vision. Read this article if you are interested in a brief overview of that history.

I am not concluding that AMD and Nvidia will trade places in the chip industry as AMD and Intel did. However, if Dr. Su repeats the feat, I would not be surprised, either. What that history does establish is that the gap between perceived dominance and actual outcomes can be wider than the market and the general public assume.

AMD’s Win Does Not Have to Be NVDA’s Loss

For my thesis to work out, it does not require AMD to become categorically superior. It only required AMD to become a viable competitor that was competitive enough, at the right time, in a market where customers were willing to adopt an alternative for the right price.

The current market narrative implicitly treats AI as a winner-take-all market. For any emerging technology, AI included, there rarely is a perpetual monopoly.

AMD does not have to match or beat Nvidia. It only needs to take sufficient market share. Concerns about Nvidia's monopolistic pricing power and overreliance upon Nvidia may push current and future hyperscalers toward diversification. Also, the demand for AI compute has expanded so dramatically that customers may have to prioritize availability over perfection. A “good enough” alternative to CUDA and Nvidia's GPU may be sufficient or even more economical. AMD is positioned exactly there to potentially take advantage of such opportunities, which may provide enough to substantially boost AMD's overall market cap.

The Bet

This is not a bet that AMD is better than Nvidia. It is a bet that Nvidia cannot maintain its absolute dominance in the long term.

Nvidia’s current valuation reflects an expectation that its dominance will translate into capturing most of the economic value of AI computing for the long term. That may prove partially true. It rarely proves entirely true.

AMD does not need to disprove Nvidia’s strength. It only needs to demonstrate that the market is large enough and competitive enough that a second player can emerge on a meaningful scale despite the advantage enjoyed by Nvidia now.

Part of my thinking here is influenced by Chris Miller’s book Chip War, which I recommend every investor of NVDA and AMD to read. One theme that comes up repeatedly is that leadership in semiconductors rarely stays fixed for as long as people expect. Companies that look dominant at one point in time often lose that position when technology shifts or when a different part of the value chain becomes more important.

That does not mean Nvidia will follow the same path. But it does make me a bit more cautious about assuming that today’s advantage will remain intact for a very long time. For example, a challenger’s smaller scale may turn into an advantage, enabling it to adopt new technologies more swiftly to unseat the incumbent leader. After all, the technology field evolves faster than expected or can be foreseen.

I speculate if anyone had asked Jensen Huang ten years ago whether he expected to have the fortune and fame he obtained today, would he have said yes? I doubt it. Things change, often faster than we think, especially in the technology field, and even the people at the epicenter of that may not know.

What If AMD Takes 25% of the Market?

This is where the discussion should move from narrative to arithmetic. Nvidia currently generates approximately $148.5 billion in annual revenue. That alone suggests that the AI accelerator market is already extremely large. Assume, for the sake of argument, that the market grows to $300 billion over the next five years, or at a rate of about 15% annually. I do not know if this will happen, but Nvidia’s current valuation implies continued expansion, at least at such a scale.

If AMD captures 25% of that market, it would generate approximately $75 billion in revenue. Assume further that AMD achieves a conservative net margin of 25%, which is only half of Nvidia’s current level. That would result in roughly $18–19 billion in net income. Applying a 25x earnings multiple yields a valuation of approximately $450–475 billion. From a current base of about $360 billion, that represents roughly 30% upside.

If the market grows to $400 billion instead, or AMD’s net profit margin improves to 35%, the same assumptions produce approximately $25 billion in net income and a valuation of about $625 billion, implying significantly greater upside.

In short, the extent of AMD’s success depends on the size of the market and AMD’s ability to secure a more meaningful share of it than implied by the current market cap gap between AMD and NVDA.

Risks to the Thesis

The argument for AMD ultimately rests on the assumption that Nvidia will not retain the vast majority of the economics of the AI compute market. That may or may not work out to be the case in the future.

First, Nvidia’s advantage may be more durable than it appears. CUDA is an ecosystem built over many years, with deep integration into developer workflows and AI frameworks. Even if AMD’s hardware performance approaches parity, the friction involved in switching away from CUDA may remain high enough to discourage meaningful migration. That is to say that the assumption that large customers will diversify suppliers may not apply.

Second, the valuation framework presented depends heavily on the future size of the AI accelerator market. While current growth rates are extraordinary, it is not clear that they are sustainable at scale. A slowdown in AI spending would disproportionately affect AMD’s upside, as its investment case relies on such future expansion at least as much as Nvidia does.

Finally, at the risk of stating the obvious, it is hard to catch up in any race with such a wide gap, and AMD cannot afford any execution blunder. While I think AMD may have meaningful upside under certain scenarios, I am not blessed with enough uncommon insights—nor foolishly confident enough—to conclude that the path to realizing that upside is simple or assured.

Conclusion

Nvidia is an extraordinary company. Its platform, ecosystem, and execution justify its leadership position. But investing is not about identifying the best company. By the time it is publicly recognized to be the best company, its stock will be priced to reflect that consensus, and its potential to deliver market-beating returns diminishes dramatically. It is about identifying the next potential winner whose competitive merits are likely underappreciated.

What I am trying to argue here is that if I were forced to choose between NVDA and AMD, I would be inclined to invest in AMD. NVDA may be a good short- to intermediate-term investment for the investors who understand its competitive position well enough to know when to exit from it. However, it probably is a SELL for the long-term investor. AMD, on the other hand, will probably deliver a better risk-adjusted return as an intermediate- to long-term investment.

In short, my thesis really is that the probability for NVDA to continue its dominance in the long run should be smaller than implied in its stock price today.

Conversely, the probability for AMD to continue to lag so far behind is also smaller than implied in its current stock price. AMD is not required to beat NVDA in the race. It only needs to survive to run the race reasonably well until the day it somehow closes the gap against NVDA. And if it does, its stock would perform much better relative to the stock of NVDA.

For the above reasons, I rate NVDA a SELL and AMD a modest BUY.

P.S.: I do not have any position in either AMD or NVDA, or any AI and related technology company. I readily admit that analyzing stocks in those rapidly evolving fields is way too challenging. To that end, this article is less of an analysis of the business fundamentals behind those two stocks, but more of a note on the challenges of investing in technology stocks in the long term.

data.title: Rates Spark: The EUR Curve Is At A Delicate Balance
data.description:
By Michiel Tukker, Senior UK & Eurozone Rates Strategist | Benjamin Schroeder, Senior Rates Strategist

Longer EUR rates cannot keep up if oil pushes much higher

Hopes of a near-term resolution to the Middle East conflict are fading again, while growth concerns are still intensifying. Brent oil pricing well above $100 per barrel is too high for EUR rates markets to find comfort. The very front end of the swap curve has been sticking close to the script since the start of the war: a $10 rise implying almost an entire hike extra over the next 12 months. And with European Central Bank officials giving little pushback to the hawkish pricing, we don’t see why markets would deviate from this trading strategy.

But the story has been different further out the curve, where growth concerns have started to push downwards at higher oil prices. When we zoom into the 2s5s part of the curve, we see a non-linearity arising for higher oil prices. If the pattern were to hold, oil pushing beyond $120 poses the risk of a steep inversion. In this scenario, rate markets see steep ECB rate hikes followed up by a significant easing cycle on the back of deteriorating growth.

For higher oil prices longer rates struggle keeping up with the short end

However, another narrative is gaining traction as of late, whereby energy disruptions will last much longer than initially feared. And ECB President Christine Lagarde has fuelled such concerns with her latest interview in The Economist. She remarked that risks from the war are being underestimated and that the ECB's technical experts believe that damage already incurred to the infrastructure was enough to disrupt the energy supply for years rather than just months. Laying out the spectrum of possible responses just earlier this week, she said, "large, sustained deviations call for forceful monetary policy action."

Oil prices pushed rates higher over the course of Thursday. The front end is back to pricing more than three ECB hikes this year. But this time, the curve out to the 10y wasn't lagging behind any more – 2y to 10y rates all ended the day 11bp higher.

Friday’s events and market view

The oil impact moves into focus with Spain the first eurozone country to release flash CPI data for March. The headline year-on-year rate is expected to jump to 3.6% from 2.3% in February. But the ECB will also be watching the underlying eurozone inflation, which had nudged higher in February to 2.4% YoY, and markets currently still expect it to dip back to 2.3% next week. For Spain, that core figure is expected to rise to 2.8% from 2.7% today, though.

The ECB will also be releasing its consumer survey of inflation expectations. While this is for February, markets see them nudging higher on both the 1y and 3y horizon.

In the US, the market will look at the final University of Michigan Consumer Index for March, which should now cover more responses coming in during the turmoil.

Central bank speakers will also remain key. From the ECB, Isabel Schnabel is expected to speak again. From the Fed, Tom Barkin, Mary Daly and Anna Paulson are slated for the day.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

data.title: VIGI Upgrade: From Underperformance To Portfolio Stability
data.description:
I issued a sell call on the Vanguard International Dividend Appreciation Index Fund ETF (VIGI) in June last year, at a time when the US growth markets looked decidedly a better place to be. The call has aged well, with VIGI showing a total return of negative 1%, while the S&P 500 has returned close to 10% (including recent corrections).

The ~11% relative underperformance reinforces the core premise of my earlier thesis - VIGI was structurally disadvantaged versus US equities and unlikely to deliver competitive returns. However, the sell call seems too harsh given the absolute performance has not been disastrous. In current conditions, I feel this Sell rating underweights the role of VIGI as a portfolio stabilizer rather than a return engine. In this thesis, I re-evaluate VIGI for the road ahead and argue it is better rated as a Hold than the more aggressive Sell I had in June last year.

Reviewing The Old Thesis

The earlier thesis had found that long-term returns were not competitive (looking at historical data) across both US growth and value benchmarks. That weakness remains, but does not support a sell if portfolio stabilization is the anchor (not returns). The dividend yield also does not qualify VIGI as a strong income fund. Both these factors still imply VIGI is not yet buy material, but definitely do not rule out a hold.

Other factors such as correlation and drawdown protection do not show up strongly either. But they need an update. In terms of correlation, VIGI did show the desired resilience in the mid-2025 US drawdown, and overall correlations have been lower since (less than 0.8 and over a sustained period of time, down from ~0.9 before the April 2025 sell-off).

I was also wary of the geographical tilt in slower growth economies, especially Japan (deflation, weak wage growth), Switzerland (currency drag), and Canada (low growth momentum). While a lot of that concern remains, the Japan thesis needs an upgrade. The Japan market is undergoing a structural revival driven by corporate governance reforms and record buybacks. You can read here why I have become bullish on Japan, and that stand is working out well so far.

And finally, the Sell rating was a relative call on US growth. That has changed considerably. With a range of issues that point to a slower 2026 for US growth markets - from geopolitical uncertainty to stagflation risks to AI trades cooling due to landscape uncertainties (like in the SaaS space), capex pressures, and lower appetite for long duration assumptions - VIGI's lower volatility becomes a more meaningful factor now.

What Is Already Changing

We have already seen how defensive portfolios can catch up with growth quite fast even without growth collapsing. In another article I wrote comparing SPYD and SPY, I observed how SPYD outperformed SPY despite strong growth markets at that time. And all it took was a growth consolidation (not a crash) for rotation to work out. The SPYD versus SPY picture has since changed, and markets have corrected further since, but the key takeaway is the power of rotation away from US growth, even when it simply consolidated (time corrects) after years of growth. That rotation push should help VIGI too - maybe not a Buy but a surer Hold.

Since I am now viewing VIGI as a portfolio stabilizer, there are a few developments that position it better than what it looked like in June last year. One, the emerging market exposure has come down, which means greater stability - and while in the long term that is at the cost of return potential, right now it is not sacrificing a lot because several emerging economies like India are reeling from the effects of a weaker currency that is keeping markets under pressure. Plus, the higher allocation to Japan (~30% versus ~27% in June 2025) more than makes up for the lost return potential in my view.

Secondly, the sector allocation has also moved meaningfully away from tech and assigned higher weights to financials, industrials, and healthcare (now accounting for over 60% of the portfolio). In my view, that aligns VIGI better with the current environment, where sectors that are more tied to earnings delivery, capital returns, and real economic activity provide better diversification away from tech, where long duration assumptions are being challenged. Plus, valuations in these sectors have run up less than tech valuations in the past couple of years - that is something that does not matter for a total return portfolio, but does so for a portfolio stabilizer. Tech allocation in VIGI has shrunk visibly from ~15% in June last year to less than 10% now. The higher weight in financials also looks good, as rates are increasingly looking to stay higher for longer, and that should help financials deliver earnings and capital return as long as credit stress doesn't start showing up. The portfolio shift also overall supports a higher yield - still not a buy based on income appeal alone, but it adds to the hold thesis. Since the time of my June thesis, VIGI's TTM yield has improved significantly from ~1.8% to ~2.3% - material improvement for its historical yield profile.

An Upgrade to a Hold

This VIGI thesis supports a Hold rating, although for much of the thesis I had to highlight the positives to justify the upgrade. The Sell rating appears harsh in the current context, but that doesn't mean VIGI is a Buy either. I do believe diversification and portfolio stabilization are the needs of the hour. And that rotation will benefit ex-US and non-growth themes overall. At least the case to diversify away from growth at this point is a better risk-return adjusted positioning, if not from a total returns lens alone - the lens that had led to VIGI's Sell rating.

However, that play does have some alternatives over VIGI that could be better overweight candidates. VIGI's defensive profile is not really very robust (as we saw in April 2025 last year during a widespread tariff sell-off), and its returns are not lucrative. The income thesis is strengthened now, but it is still not an income buy, looking at the yield levels on an absolute basis. Alternatives like SPYD, VXUS and VYMI could be better equipped to play the rotation thesis than VIGI and you can read about my views on those here, here and here. SPYD could double up as a portfolio stabilizer and an income vehicle. VXUS adds the international diversification that helps in the current scenario (and still yields slightly higher than VIGI). VYMI could also help diversify away from growth, provide income, and provide the portfolio stabilization that VIGI does. VIGI could be part of the overall portfolio too, but at lower weights. The thesis has become stronger than it was in June 2025, but there is a lot of ground left to cover before it can be considered for significant allocations within a balanced portfolio.

data.title: ThredUp: Strong Thrifting Demand Underlies Falling Valuation
data.description:
For the past few weeks, it has felt as if the stock market has kept falling due to the same news: escalating tensions in Iran and a relatively weaker macroeconomy that has made the Q4 earnings season less optimistic than we had hoped. Amid this backdrop, however, I continue to emphasize single stock selection and active portfolio management as the core way to beat the markets. In my view, many attractive value-buying opportunities have opened up in the recent slide.

ThredUp (TDUP), an online thrifting and resale platform, is one such stock. ThredUp had a banner year in 2025 as investors pounced on its huge growth rates, but since January, the stock has lost more than 40% of its value. ThredUp's growth rates are moderating downward, but at the same time its bottom-line multiples are heading toward a more reasonable spot. The question for investors now is, does ThredUp have more to lose, or is now the right time to buy the dip?

I last wrote a "Buy" article on ThredUp in November, when the stock was trading in the low $7s. Needless to say, I did not predict the SaaSpocalypse, the rising geopolitical conflicts, and all the other macro-driven factors that have sparked a massive selloff in growth stocks since the start of 2026. At the same time, such a steep decline in any stock warrants a look with fresh eyes. When I analyze ThredUp again from a blank slate, I find more appeal in the stock in the $3s and at more reasonable adjusted EBITDA multiples than I did in the past. I'm reiterating my "Buy" rating here.

Two factors are worth mentioning upfront in defending the buy case for ThredUp today. The first is that while ThredUp has hit some near-term snags in terms of adjusted EBITDA margin, the company is still on track for long-term expansion in adjusted EBITDA that makes its current multiples feel reasonable. At current share prices near $3, ThredUp trades at a market cap of $431.9 million. After we net off the $48.1 million of cash and $18.2 million of debt on ThredUp's most recent balance sheet, the company's resulting enterprise value is $402.0 million.

As shown in the chart above, for FY26, ThredUp is guiding to $349-$355 million in revenue this year, which reflects 13% y/y midpoint growth. It's also expecting a 6.0% adjusted EBITDA margin that implies $21.1 million of adjusted EBITDA, which is 56% adjusted EBITDA growth and 160 bps of y/y margin expansion.

ThredUp's valuation sits at 19.0x EV/FY26 adjusted EBITDA. For a company that is still growing at a mid-teens pace, is achieving a >50% adjusted EBITDA growth rate through both top-line growth and margin expansion, and is one of the leaders in its very small retail niche, I think the company sits in very buyable territory.

The other factor to note is that ThredUp is in the midst of substantially expanding its market opportunity through emphasizing its "resale as a service" feature. ThredUp began as a pure thrift and donation marketplace, essentially a C2C business in which individual customers sold their used items to others. Resale, however, is the larger $45 billion slice (61%) of the company's $74 billion TAM in 2029.

Resale, however, is different. Resale helps retailers turn around returns and unwanted items to a whole new set of customers, potentially improving their sell-through and inventory turnover rates. For ThredUp, it unlocks a new channel for higher-quality, premium-branded supply. As the company strikes more resale partners, we expect the company to be able to accelerate its revenue growth rates as the addressable market grows.

All in all, while the choppy macroeconomy certainly does increase operating risk for ThredUp, I see no reason for the massive selloff in this stock, and I'm content to leverage the dip as a buying opportunity.

Q4 download

Let's now go through ThredUp's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q4 earnings summary is shown below:

ThredUp's revenue grew 18% y/y to $79.7 million, beating Wall Street's expectations of $77.2 million (+15% y/y) by a three-point margin. We do note, however, that ThredUp's revenue growth did decelerate sharply from 34% y/y revenue growth in Q3.

Management posits that the shakier macroeconomy could end up being a tailwind for ThredUp in a number of ways. Tariffs on new production could increase the appeal of buying secondhand; in addition, more consumers could be incentivized to monetize their closets to generate extra income. Per CEO James Reinhart's remarks on the Q4 earnings call:

Before we dive deeper into our strategy for 2026, I want to address the broader consumer landscape. I've said for a number of quarters that I think the American consumer may be weaker than headline data would appear to indicate and that recent data validates some of the [indiscernible]. In 2025, job growth was anemic with the DLS revising numbers down by the largest factor in 20 years. At the same time, the New York Fed confirmed that nearly 90% of the 2025 tariff burden fell directly on firms and consumers. This is on top of an affordability crisis where nondiscretionary costs like rents and insurance have structurally reset at higher levels, effectively shrinking the wallet share left over for apparel and other discretionary goods.

All this taken together, I think it's fair to say that the macroeconomic environment for discretionary spending remains uncertain, and the American consumers understandably approaching the year with a degree of caution. However, we believe these circumstances allow us to offer a differentiated approach. While traditional apparel retailers may face headwinds in a value-driven environment, our managed marketplace is uniquely built to capture upside demand as consumers prioritize both the stretch of their dollar and the liquidity in their closets.

When we take a step back from the current quarter, we are impressed with the progress that ThredUp has been able to make in the past year. In FY25, the company's base of active buyers shot up 30% y/y to 1.65 million, reflecting a rapid resurgence in the popularity of thrift and resale. Total orders also grew 25% y/y to 6.1 million, as the company also benefited from a slew of loyal repeat buyers.

Where the company slipped up slightly in Q4 was on profitability. In Q4, adjusted EBITDA of $2.9 million fell 41% y/y, with adjusted EBITDA margins of 3.7% falling 370 bps y/y and 90 bps sequentially versus Q3.

The company attributed the decay to higher growth spending throughout the quarter; however, the outperformance in orders resulting from those investments didn't happen until later in the quarter.

We note that ThredUp is still making progress toward its longer-term profit goals. As shown below, relative to the company's 4.4% adjusted EBITDA margin in FY25, the company has a longer-term target of hitting 20%-25% adjusted EBITDA margins.

Its gross margin profile is already essentially at target, thanks to the company's shift to a consignment-first business model without taking on inventory. The remainder of the margin upside is due to come from scaling down operational and technology expenses as a percentage of revenue, which should naturally come with scale (especially as ThredUp increases its resale initiatives).

Key takeaways

ThredUp is an excellent buy-the-dip play as the company continues to refine its business model, expanding its supply to include resale partners and optimizing its margin profile. The onset of tariffs and a weaker macroeconomy may continue to fuel demand away from new items and into resale/thrift, further benefiting ThredUp's growth rates. Stay long here.

data.title: Pony AI Q4 Earnings: Early Signs Of Commercialization Amid Global Expansion
data.description:
Thesis

Pony AI Inc. (PONY) has just reported 4Q25 earnings. The initial viewing of the numbers was quite bad, with the stock closing about 15% lower yesterday, as I'll explain. As for operations, they're now expanding into Croatia, Qatar, the UAE, and Singapore, and they’ve set a goal of over 20 cities globally by the end of 2026. So if we take the good with the bad, I think the key question we need to answer here is whether Pony can convert its early lead in unit economics and fleet scaling into actual, durable, high-margin network effects. And they need to do it before capital intensity and competition erode any returns. The current data would suggest some strong demand, which is good. However, they’re still in a capital-intensive, execution-heavy phase where sustained profitability is really going to depend on scaling utilisation, lowering BOM costs, and stabilising their revenue mix to move away from those volatile licensing streams.

As for my continued buy case, Pony is showing some inflexion into commercialisation. Robotaxi revenue is up about 160% since last year and is growing fast. We even saw some UE break-even in key cities. In my previous coverage, I went over the road to profitability for Pony, and I feel they're still on track. I think there's a clear path to scaling to a 3,000-plus fleet, with some early signs of a viable, high-margin network business. At the same time, its global expansion strategy and ecosystem partnerships with the likes of Toyota, Uber, and Tencent are going to be key to unlocking significantly higher pricing/margins abroad. That's going to be needed to justify the current premium valuation.

FY25 earnings

Pony’s 4Q25 and FY25 are still showing us a lot of R&D-heavy experimentation, but we’re also seeing some transition into early-stage commercialisation at scale. As you know, Robotaxi is now their central growth engine. In 4Q25, total revenue came in at about $29.1 million, down 18% since last year and mainly due to a 53% decline in licensing and applications revenue. This segment was only $9.4 million compared to $20.0 million a year ago, since it had benefited from one-off projects in that prior year. However, I think this somewhat masks the bigger focus here, in that Robotaxi revenue managed to reach $6.7 million, up about 159.5% year over year, whilst their fare-based revenues alone grew over 500%. So there is clearly genuine demand on the streets, rather than pilot activity. As for Robotruck, revenue stayed pretty flat at $13.1 million, just up 1.2%. For FY25, total revenue hit $90.0 million, a 20% jump from FY24, with Robotaxi contributing about $16.6 million to that sum. Its contribution is up 128.6% since last year, which is impressive.

In terms of operational KPIs, we’re seeing support for this trajectory, with fleet size reaching 1,446 vehicles and a target of 3,000 put in place. Per-vehicle economics are also improving; there’s about a RMB394 or roughly $56, peak daily net revenue and about 25 rides per vehicle per day. The company also reported UE breakeven in Shenzhen and Guangzhou, which is a pretty critical milestone that tells me that, at sufficient density and utilisation, the model can be economically viable.

As for profitability metrics, I think they need some careful interpretation since, on initial viewing, they can look quite poor. For 4Q25, gross profit declined to just $3.7 million compared to $7.5 million last year, whilst gross margin compressed to about 12.7%, from 21.0%. Now, we have to keep in mind that this was mainly due to a much higher mix of lower-margin Robotruck revenue. Operating expenses also fell pretty sharply to $77.6 million, down 57% year over year, which we can put down to the absence of IPO-related share-based compensation. However, on a normalised basis, non-GAAP opex actually increased about 25% to $69.6 million from some scaling efforts and more Gen-7 R&D. So this resulted in a non-GAAP operating loss of about $65.9 million, and goes to show that underlying losses are still widening with the company investing ahead of revenue. That's also one of the main reasons for the hefty 15% sell-off yesterday.

On the other hand, Pony reported GAAP net income of $75.5 million in 4Q25, but it’s worth mentioning that this was largely due to $132.5 million in fair value gains on trading securities. So that makes it non-recurring. The much more relevant figure here, in my opinion, is the non-GAAP net loss of $49.0 million, which deteriorated year over year. On a full-year basis, the company posted a hefty non-GAAP net loss of $174.0 million compared to $132.3 million last year, so at scale is still quite distant.

The balance sheet, however, does give us some positives. They managed to end FY25 with about $1.51 billion in cash, equivalents, and investments, up quite a bit from $587.7 million in 3Q25 after their Hong Kong IPO. This should give them a pretty substantial runway to fund their aggressive expansion. We can actually already see that in the negative free cash flow of $208.8 million for the year. Strategically, here, management is prioritising scaling through partnerships, with the big deal coming from Toyota. They secured about 1,000 vehicles for Gen-7 deployment, but this should also come with some geographic diversification.

Data moat

We’ve also heard from management that they view their competitive advantage as a deep AI and data moat rather than a pure mobility play. So in that sense, it would be centred around two core pillars. The first of which would be their proprietary world model, or PonyWorld, the second their real-world fleet data feedback loop. If we start with their world model, it’s designed to generalise across complex driving environments, which is a very essential requirement for Level 4 autonomy. Management actually stressed on the call that this is fundamentally different from L2 ADAS systems offered by traditional OEMs. You see, while L2 systems assist drivers, L4 requires solving that last 1% of edge cases in rare, unpredictable scenarios. This would be the hardest technical barrier and a key differentiator. The classic example here is a pedestrian suddenly stepping into the road while pushing a bicycle, at night and in heavy rain. If there are also partially obscured lane markings, and an oncoming vehicle is swerving slightly, then L4 would need this scenario to be handled adequately.

Management’s take is that a growing fleet, already over 1,400 vehicles and scaling more toward 3,000, should create a hefty compounding data advantage. So the more real-world miles will improve the model, which then, in turn, improves safety and performance. Somewhat of a flywheel effect, in which it becomes very difficult for new entrants to replicate. At the same time, the company is also making pretty tangible progress on cost structure. With the Gen-7 Robotaxi platform, the design is being done around significantly lower bill-of-materials (BOM) costs. They’ve actually already achieved progress with this on the Gen-4 Robotruck that saw about a 70% BOM cost reduction. So that's a nice step toward commercial viability. Management also has some targets in place for an additional 20% BOM reduction in 2026. So if we put that alongside operational efficiencies such as the lower insurance costs and improved remote operations, with fewer human interventions per vehicle, things do seem to be heading in the right direction.

Valuation

As for the valuation, Pony is clearly being priced as a very high-growth, pre-profit autonomous driving platform. The EV to sales ratio sits at 46.9x trailing and 51.1x forward, and the price to sales at 38.0 trailing. All of which are dramatically above the sector median near the 3x range, which would imply a premium of over 1,000%. So clearly, the market is pricing in a steep ramp in Robotaxi revenues along with successful scaling to thousands of vehicles. In other words, the current valuation assumes Pony can transition from $90 million annual revenue today to a multi-billion-dollar revenue base over time, with some improving unit economics already hinted at by breakeven in a couple of key cities.

The more constructive angle would come from the balance sheet and asset backing. From that viewpoint, things look much more attractive with the price-to-book standing at 5.5x trailing and 3.5x forward. These are notably much closer to sector norms and actually even below the sector median on a forward basis. As you know, this comes from Pony's pretty strong capital position at over $1.5 billion in cash and investments post-IPO relative to their scale. So this does reduce the downside risk somewhat and should also help fund this aggressive expansion without any near-term dilution risk.

The way I see it, you’re effectively paying a premium over book for proprietary AV technology, data advantage, and future network scale, but not at extreme levels relative to some of the other early-stage tech platforms. Overall, the valuation here tells me the market is willing to overlook those near-term losses and cash burn in exchange for a very dominant position in a potentially massive autonomous mobility market. However, this is going to leave very little margin for execution missteps, especially when we take into consideration how far current revenue multiples already discount future growth.

Looking ahead to international expansion

For quite some time, a pretty big pillar of Pony’s long-term investment case has been a deliberate shift toward a globally distributed ecosystem. On the OEM side, the Toyota partnership is quite significant here. Not only does it give them just supply access, but they’re getting deep integration that could potentially accelerate fleet deployment while lowering manufacturing and integration risk. What I mean by this is that Toyota isn’t just selling those cars, but they’re also helping design vehicles optimised for autonomy. And, at the same time, partnerships with Uber, Tencent, and Bolt/Stellantis should give Pony some positioning to layer the autonomous stack on top of those already existing demand networks rather than building everything from scratch. That point is important if they aim to scale utilisation quickly.

It’s a somewhat ecosystem approach that’s directly tied to management’s international strategy going forward. They’re set on over 20 cities globally by the end of 2026, with about 50% being located outside China. So this should include some early moves into Croatia, Qatar, the UAE, and Singapore. The rationale here would then be explicitly margin-driven, and management has actually acknowledged that fare pricing in China is structurally low. All we have to do is look at that $56 peak daily net revenue, which is extremely low for an average of 25 rides per day. Those overseas markets offer much higher pricing power and better unit economics. So that global expansion isn’t just about growth but rather profitability uplift at scale.

For investors, going forward, I think that a large portion of Pony’s future margin expansion is going to be contingent on successful execution abroad, not just domestic scaling. So, management signaling that Pony’s endgame is not a China-centric robotaxi operator, but a globally integrated autonomous mobility platform would align with this. Partnerships are there to help reduce capital intensity, and international markets are there to drive higher returns. What’s left for Pony is a lot of execution complexity, regulatory risk, and dependency on partners. So it makes global rollout a very critical variable in whether that current premium valuation I mentioned can be justified.

data.title: SoFi: Execution Makes It A Buy Again (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
SoFi (SOFI) released full-year 2025 results recently. I revisited this stock to catch up and because I've seen a lot of discussion about the quality of its loans. I felt more positive, though, so I am here to upgrade.

Summary of Previous Theses

I originally covered SOFI in July 2024. It was ranging from $6 to $7 per share back then. This was a lot lower than the period after its IPO.

Looking at the chart above, it had fallen from highs in the $20s. I thought things were going well, however. I mean, they finally had profitable quarters.

In 2022, they acquired Golden Pacific, which got them a bank charter. I was skeptical at first, but I was proved wrong. The $22M acquisition had created $500M in savings on interest expense because they could use deposits instead. I thought earnings could reach $1.5B in five to ten years. I also thought market cap expansion would pull the share price up to $12-$15. So I rated it a buy.

I liked a lot of what SoFi was doing. It was creating more sources of revenue. It wasn't depending on student loans as much. It created a burgeoning tech platform that actually partners with other financial institutions.

BUT, stock was at $17 then, and I felt it had caught up to my valuation. Because of that, I changed my mind and re-rated it Hold.

Full-Year 2025 Results

Quite a bit of time has passed since I covered SOFI, and the financial results show that a lot has changed since 2024.

Net revenue grew from $2,675M in 2024 to $3,613M in 2025. This was a consequence of gains in both net interest income and noninterest income. This is important, given SoFi's emphasis on their dual nature of banking/lending and serving as a tech platform for financial partners. On that note, let's consider revenue when broken down among their three reportable segments:

  • Lending
  • Technology Platform
  • Financial Services

All segments enjoyed growth, but financial services was the fastest ($821M to $1,542M), and it demonstrated a very strong contribution profit margin.

The Financial Services segment leverages the flexibility of their mobile-first business model, creating many tuck-in products that go beyond their initial loan product (historically, student loans). These include personal loans and lines of credit, yes, but it has expanded to include investing, payments, and more recently crypto services.

The Technology Platform segment, effectively, leverages SoFi's infrastructure into a product that SoFi can contract out to their partners. While it makes sense that they would grow their in-house services more, this is a nice way to capture extra revenue.

When looking at other metrics that indicate the size of SoFi's business, the higher revenue across these segments is unsurprising. Total members grew from 10.1M in 2024 to 13.7M in 2025. The number of the financial products in use also grew from 14.7M to 20.2M in that period.

In spite of growth on the top line, SoFi reported a slight decline in net income: from $498.7M in 2024 to $481.3M in 2025. While noninterest expense grew about $600M, this was sufficiently covered by the $1B gain in net revenue. Rather, the biggest impact was that 2024 included a net tax benefit, while they incurred taxes in 2025. Income before taxes grew from $233.3M to $525.9M, indicating that the underlying earnings power grew substantially.

The balance sheet mostly showed the growth as its banking and lending are concerned. Total deposits grew by about $11.2B, supporting a $10.5B increase in total loans on the balance sheet.

This helped to reduce their debt from $3.1B to $1.8B and to grow their cash balance. This occurred in late December, at $27.50 per share. SoFi took advantage of the higher valuation, rather than being forced to issue equity like a distressed business.

In summary, it was a very good year for SoFi, and the various, moving pieces that I discussed reflect how it is maturing and executing on its vision of being a more well-rounded fintech.

Outlook and Valuation

Let's consider a few numbers. In 2024, I opined that SoFi could reach $1.5B in earnings in five to ten years. At that time, earnings were annualizing to about $400M. 2026's guidance of adjusted net income, per the latest earnings call, was $825M. This is showing tremendous progress on their execution. Not only that, management guided a net revenue CAGR of 30% from 2025 to 2028, with an EPS CAGR of 38% to 42%.

That suggests that SoFi can hit that $1.5B milestone. It's optimistic guidance, so we should assess the growth drivers first.

The first one to assess is their crypto product. As that was introduced in 2025, I believe we will see it use cases develop in the coming years, particularly now that the GENIUS Act is passed and the consequences this has for banks. As CEO Anthony Noto explained in the earnings call:

Since March, when the OCC made crypto permissible for national banks, we've moved with urgency to bring new products to our members. In October, we enhanced our unprecedented money movement offering with the launch of SoFi Pay, our first payment product that leverages blockchain technology to provide fast, seamless, low cost and safe international payments.

I believe SoFi's tech-friendly, innovative roots position it well to utilize crypto and blockchain technology, not simply as a retail gimmick but for actual use cases that create value for members.

Other new services that were launched in 2025 (such as an AI coach and international remittances) will be important to watch. For SoFi to maintain such high growth, they will need to increase revenue per member, while also continuing to gain members. Adoption of the newer products in the upcoming quarters will give hints of that.

Given their swift execution and adaptation in the last two years, I think the next goal to consider is if SoFi can do $3B in earnings in five to ten years. If they continue to perform as guided, they will hit this. SoFi is currently a $21.3B market cap near $16.50 per share. If they simply maintain a P/E of 20, that's a near-triple. In short, I believe SoFi can reach $45 per share.

Risk Factors

Initially, some of my concern was their ability to compete effectively with other banks and financial institutions. The last two years show that they compete very well, even being something of a pioneer. Rather, I think the main risks come back to the underwriting portion of the revenue, which somewhat explains why they've diversified into more fee-based revenues.

While personal loans have grown and now make up a majority of the loan book, student loans still accounted for 36% of their $35.8B in loans. It's a peculiar balance sheet for this reason. Student loans are, by their nature, unsecured. Young people don't have a lot of assets, and lenders can't just repossess a degree.

Most of the personal loans are unsecured too, but there are some key differences from the student product. Personal has smaller balances, shorter durations, and higher average coupons (13.11% vs. students' 5.87%). While the default rate is higher, it isn't significantly higher and is adequately addressed by the higher coupon.

Getting to the point, SoFi was formed in 2011, after the 2008 financial crisis. Its core product has never been subject to a similar stress test as that of subprime mortgages (which even had the benefit of collateral). If the stress test happens, a lot of these student loans can quickly become worthless.

Personal loans have this same problem, really. SoFi can tighten credit and let loans run off in preparation for a distress, but mortgages or secured business loans are not present.

Conclusion

SoFi continues to innovate and win business in what is a competitive space. 2025 showed their ability to integrate new products and earn their relevance. I have come to view SoFi as something of a universal fintech, similar to how I view Microsoft (MSFT) in comparison to other Mag7 stocks.

The main thing that could hold them back is that (mobile-friendly as they are) they don't have a lot of collateral to collect if loans default. One can doubt the margin of safety being sufficient as this stock rises into the $20s, but as it's well below that, I upgrade to Buy in recognition of their continually improving business.

data.title: Columbia Select Mid Cap Growth Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Fund performance

  • Institutional Class shares of Columbia Select Mid Cap Growth Fund returned -3.51% for the period ending December 31, 2025.
  • The fund's benchmark, the Russell Midcap Growth Index, returned -3.70% during the same period.
  • The fund performed essentially in line with the Russell Mid Cap Growth Index for the quarter. Stock selection within information technology and health care, along with an overweight to materials, led positive contributions to relative performance, while selection within financials, industrials and consumer discretionary weighed most heavily on returns.

Market overview

The tepid returns for mid-cap equities came despite better-than-expected economic growth, robust corporate earnings results, and inflation that largely remained below an annualized rate of 3%. The U.S. Federal Reserve continued to ease policy, cutting rates by a quarter point at its meetings in October and December and announcing an end to the multi-year effort to reduce the size of its balance sheet. Stocks also remained supported by ongoing excitement surrounding the artificial intelligence (AI) theme, albeit with a brief stretch of concern in November that AI-related equities were in a "bubble."

Within the mid-cap universe, growth and value stocks returned -3.70% and 1.42%, as gauged by the Russell Midcap Growth and Russell Midcap Value indices, respectively.

Top holdings (% of net assets): as of December 31, 2025

Take-Two Interactive Software (TTWO) 3.06
Carpenter Technology (CRS) 2.95
Howmet Aerospace (HWM) 2.89
Mongodb (MDB) 2.85
Spotify Technology (SPOT) 2.76
Doordash (DASH) 2.70
Celsius Holdings (CELH) 2.52
RBC Bearings (RBC) 2.43
Sharkninja (SN) 2.31
DraftKings-CI A (DKNG) 2.29
Top holdings exclude short-term holdings and cash, if applicable. Fund holdings are as of the date given, are subject to change at any time, and are not recommendations to buy or sell any security.

Quarterly portfolio recap

Contributors to relative performance

Carpenter Technology is a metallurgical company that produces superalloys used in the “hot” sections of aircraft engines. The stock advanced on record quarterly profitability, strong free-cash-flow generation and confident fiscal year 2026 guidance, supported by robust demand within the aerospace and power-generation markets. Carpenter posted earnings beats and raised guidance throughout 2025, which was the most profitable year in its history. The company remains positioned to benefit from tight specialty metal alloy markets, which have led to pricing strength and structurally higher margins. These tailwinds stand to intensify in 2026, as Boeing begins to ramp up production of commercial airplanes.

MongoDB provides an open-source Not Only SQL (NoSQL) document database that stores data in document form within collections, rather than in traditional tables composed of columns and rows. The stock came under pressure in early 2025 on concerns around perceived competition within the AI space. However, MongoDB’s shares moved sharply higher over the last few months of the year, as it became clear that the flexibility offered by its product is relevant to AI applications, which rely on unstructured real-world data as opposed to information presented in tables; this drove strong sales of MongoDB’s Atlas cloud offering.

Sandisk is a leading supplier of flash memory storage products including solid-state drives (SSDs). Historically, hard disk drives (HDDs) have been the preferred storage solution within data centers, including AI data centers. However, with the increased demand from AI data centers, HDDs are essentially out of stock and their prices have skyrocketed. As a result, the AI data center market has pivoted to SSDs, driving their prices up as well and creating a new market opportunity for Sandisk.

Detractors from relative performance

AeroVironment (AVAV) was historically a supplier of drones, but underwent a radical transformation with the acquisition of Blue Halo, expanding its product offerings to include satellite communications, high-powered lasers and space technologies. The stock rose sharply for much of 2025 on exceptionally strong results. However, AeroVironment shares moved steadily lower beginning in mid-October, as it became clear that the U.S. government shutdown would significantly impact the timing of various contracts. We maintain a positive view on the stock, as AeroVironment is well positioned to benefit from rising global demand for drones and remote warfare solutions.

Spotify Technology is a leading music streaming service that has expanded its product offerings to include audiobooks and video podcasts, while growing its subscriber base and demonstrating strong pricing power. After a run of strong performance, the stock has come under pressure from profit-taking. We maintain a positive view on Spotify, given its resilient subscription model that should largely insulate the company from weaker consumer sentiment in a potential economic downturn.

Targa Resources (TRGP) is a midstream pipeline company focused on natural-gas liquids. The company delivered record earnings and strong capital returns in 2025, benefiting from its pipeline exposures to the Permian Basin in western Texas, which has led U.S. onshore production. The stock has stalled in recent quarters as growth expectations for the company normalized. In addition, investors began to take profits on Targa as the supply of oil and associated natural gas has increased relative to demand, leading to an easing in drilling activity.

Outlook

While we maintain a strong focus on individual stock selection, we of course monitor the macroeconomic backdrop, with an eye toward the potential impact on the companies in which we invest. In this vein, we see some potential tailwinds for company performance and investor risk sentiment in the coming year. Inflation has been more contained than originally feared in some quarters. In addition, some provisions in the tax and budget bill signed in July should provide a boost to consumer sentiment, and while there have been some signs of softening employment on the margins, layoffs have been measured and contained, while wage growth has remained reasonable. While advances from the ongoing investment in AI will take some time to spread throughout the economy, we expect the ultimate productivity enhancements to enable a higher rate of non-inflationary growth, which could allow the Fed to maintain rates at relatively low levels by historical standards.

We view the environment heading into 2026 as supportive of our focus on adding value by identifying the best growth opportunities in the mid-cap space. The core tenet of our process is to focus on identifying innovators and durable business models, products and services. More broadly, we continue to look for companies in growing markets that take market share as they generate high or rising returns on investment. Such companies will grow earnings and cash flow faster than revenue. Our intrinsic value approach helps us determine which stocks to choose for total opportunity and risk/reward. We are finding numerous opportunities as innovation drives changes, and well-positioned companies can expand profits and ultimately free cash flow. Many of these mid-cap growth stocks are attractively valued relative to their large-cap counterparts and have been overlooked by investors for some time.
